sources.citation: Goodfellow et al. Deep Learning. MIT Press, 2016.
Stanford CS224N: NLP with Deep Learning, 2024.

learning_objectives: L1: Identify steps in a standard NLP preprocessing pipeline.
L1: Define tokenisation, stemming, lemmatisation, BoW, TF-IDF, and word embeddings.
L1: State the difference between BERT (encoder-only) and GPT (decoder-only).
L2: Explain why TF-IDF outperforms raw counts for document retrieval.
L2: Describe how word2vec skip-gram produces semantic vectors.
L2: Explain how BERT's masked language modelling enables transfer learning.
L2: Compare sentiment analysis, NER, and machine translation as NLP tasks.
L3: Design a text classification pipeline from raw text to trained model.
L3: Diagnose why a sentiment model fails on negation/irony and propose remediation.

concepts.title: Preprocessing Pipeline
concepts.explanation: (1) Lowercasing. (2) Tokenisation — word, subword (BPE/WordPiece), character. (3) Stop-word removal — useful for BoW, harmful for syntax-sensitive tasks (removes 'not'). (4) Stemming — fast but inaccurate. (5) Lemmatisation — accurate. Transformers use subword with minimal preprocessing.
concepts.worked_example: GPT-2 BPE on 'The café Wi-Fi is not working': ['The','café','Wi','-','Fi','is','n't','working'].

concepts.title: BoW, TF-IDF, and Embeddings
concepts.explanation: BoW: sparse count vector, ignores order/semantics. TF-IDF: TF×log(N/df_t) — upweights discriminative terms. Word embeddings: dense semantic vectors. Contextual (BERT/GPT): token vector depends on full context.
concepts.worked_example: 'the' in all 3 docs: IDF=0 → zeroed. 'chased' in 1 doc: IDF=1.099 → highest weight.

concepts.title: word2vec and GloVe
concepts.explanation: Skip-gram: predict context words from centre word within window k. Negative sampling: distinguish real pairs from noise. GloVe: trains on global co-occurrence matrix. Both produce static embeddings.
concepts.worked_example: vec(king)−vec(man)+vec(woman)≈vec(queen). Relational structure as geometric offsets.

concepts.title: Seq2Seq and Attention
concepts.explanation: Encoder-Decoder RNN: fixed context vector bottleneck. Bahdanau attention (2015): weighted sum over all encoder states at each decode step. Generalised to self-attention in Transformer (2017).
concepts.worked_example: En→Fr 'chat': attention peaks on encoder state for 'cat'. Near-diagonal for similar language pairs.

concepts.title: BERT and GPT Architectures
concepts.explanation: BERT: encoder-only, bidirectional. Pre-trained with MLM (15% masked) + NSP. Best for understanding: classification, NER, QA. GPT: decoder-only, causal (left-to-right). Pre-trained with next-token prediction. Best for generation.
concepts.worked_example: Fine-tuning BERT: BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased', num_labels=2). Only classification head is random.

concepts.title: Sentiment, NER, and MT
concepts.explanation: Sentiment: document/aspect level; challenges: negation, irony. NER: IOB tagging, BERT-NER ~92 F1 on CoNLL-2003. MT: Transformer seq2seq, BLEU >40 = high quality.
concepts.worked_example: IOB: 'Barack Obama visited Paris last June' → [B-PER,I-PER,O,B-LOC,O,B-DATE,I-DATE].

concepts.title: Bias and Limitations
concepts.explanation: Embedding bias: gender stereotypes in word2vec. Training data bias: domain mismatch. Hallucination: fluent but factually wrong. Mitigations: debiasing, diverse data, RAG, model cards.
concepts.worked_example: Negation failure: 'not boring at all' → NEGATIVE. Fix: augment with negation examples, fine-tune BERT.
